speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three, Vera Eden, June 20, 1996. How long did you stay in this children's house in Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there from about two, three weeks after we arrived. And I stayed in Theresienstadt till-- and in that particular place till October '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time, did you have contact with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Free contact with my mother. My sister followed us on her own in a different transport to Theresienstadt in three months later. And my father then came in another three months by himself. And then the whole family had-- we were living in different quarters, but we had a daily contact. In the evening, we would meet or sit on a bench and, and eat mush together or go to mother's crowded quarters and eat the mush there, or you know, stuff like this. No we were in absolute contact at all times.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the morale like at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The morale was-- well, I cannot speak for the grownups. I knew that the war now was raging in, in Europe, that the British were in and the French were in and that was about all I can identify with. I know that people were doing underground things in Theresienstadt and got shot for it. I do not know them personally, but I knew of the-- of the happenings. I knew that the SS had a Kommandatur in Theres-- in the-- in the-- on the square. The SS in Theresienstadt was not very visible. It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, they were not very visible. But there was a awful lot of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] politics going on in the grown up world, but I was not in the grown up world.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the-- in my world, we were continuing with sharing what we had or didn't have. We were close knit, very close knit. And of course, as there was that boy-- girls' children's building, then there and there was a boys' building. So we got together and we would sing. And of course, there was a big show which was done. I don't know, maybe you heard of it. It was called the, the big-- it was You must have heard of that if you interviewed some people from Theresienstadt. Explain what it is though.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is-- it was-- as far as I know, it was-- we, we did the, the-- like our home did the decoration for the stage, so we were very proud of that. And it was a musical. It was satire?

speaker: interviewer
text: Satire, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: That word, I never can get. And it was about a big bad bug, which is a brundibar, translation, which represented the Germans. Thought And the story involved in the woods and this and finishes with a big song that brundibar is killed and the world is free and everything is happy ever after. And it ran a long time, and it was really a very lovely musical. And I am sure that it is in some museum or it was even maybe redone. It could well be--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who attended the performances?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the grownups.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Germans knew about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. At one point, I thought that it was a completely hush-hush thing, and I am pretty sure it was. Because if I think back on the-- on the contents of that story, I do not think that the Germans would have approved.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how was it staged? I mean, where, where again was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was done in one of the houses in a cellar. And they made-- you know, we made out of cardboard or whatever we could scrap into some stuff, and we made the scenery. And it was a thing to be proud of. It was-- I don't know who put it toget-- like who done the music. I mean, I'm sure there was a lot of talented people all locked up there. But it was a really-- it could go on Broadway. I tell you that. It could. It was great. At least-- I am prejudiced, but it could.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else stands out in your mind from your time in Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I feel that the old people and you know, that they had it very bad, and they couldn't-- you know, they died much sooner they would have. But as I said-- and I'm going back to the same story-- if my family could have outlasted the war in Theresienstadt, we would have been a unit coming back with the old memories, bad ones, I'm sure, but then friendships like I had in, in, in a home with youngsters which are not most of them alive. It was really un-- unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now my sister was considered a grownup, so she lived with my mother. And they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she worked with her in a-- in a, well, a washing place. What did I call it? Laundry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Laundry, yes. Thank you. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] boy, at one point-- I'm very sorry for that because at one point there was an offer from the Jewish community unit there. I mean, they've got books on all the-- all, all the how it was run and who was who. I can't tell you. I do not know. I know my sister is expert on it, but I am not, and I don't intend to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you-- Jewish community offered my father but that he could get a room and the four of us could live in that room as a family in ghe-- in the ghetto. And my father was a very unusual man, and he knew that I was doing well in the children's home. And he like got us together and he said, what do you think of this idea, like us living the four of us together? And I said, well, to be quite honest, I prefer where I am living.

speaker: interviewee
text: And with that, I'm terribly sorry because that was my last chance to live for the last time as a unit, a family, under one roof. But my father, as I said, was a extremely forward-looking person or had it in him the humanity, which today it's called humanity understanding, and he said, look, if you are happy where you are, I think we should leave it that way. And we did. Of course, when I first got into the children's home-- and that was three weeks after we got into Theresienstadt, I was terribly unhappy because I was independent, but I was mama's girl. And here I was, and she had to leave me in the evening. And I would be screaming and, and crying, saying, oh mom, please, please, please don't leave me here. And this, I'm sorry for that because I must have caused her an awful lot of heartache because now, being grow-up, having three children of my own, how often did my-- did I get a sitter and the children would cry and I would go to the first telephone box and call back. And the sitter would say, oh they're fine. They are playing. But she didn't. She came-- she had to go to her accommodation that night, seeing me crying, and saying, oh my dear God, that poor child is there alone. Well, I was alone, but I had 30-- 29-- other girls plus the supervisor, and within a week, this has eased completely. But I'm sorry that I caused her that problem. She had enough problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: In October of '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? The, the transports were going, and they were going. And we have been called up as a family, no questions asked, and we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said the transports were going. So what--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were, I think, three in front of us. I think we were the fourth one. I'm not sure. I would have to look into, into-- I think we were the fourth one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now did you have any idea where these transports were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody did. And you know, they were so very, very stupid. My father was called an invalid, OK? And in Theresienstadt, he was advised-- would-- I mean, to this day, I cannot believe it, and it's true. He was advised-- before the transport, we were called up, and I don't know who was the advisor, but it must have been somebody from the-- from the-- who, who was doing the transports. And he was told the first thing he should do where he arrives, wherever it is-- because they did not know-- as far as I know, he didn't-- to go to the commandant and tell him that he is disabled.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now can you imagine that? Of course, they would have-- as soon as they stripped him of his clothes, they would have found out. There is no point in saying that he could have been saved. He couldn't. But the point that in Theresienstadt somebody who was in the upper echelon, as far as I understood, of knowledge where these transport were supposed to go, to give a man an advice like that is a beyond my imagination. How were you notified about the transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, jeez. I really don't know. I think they just came to your accommodation and they called you up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what happened when you went to the transport.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was as a youth, everybody was allowed to take one suitcase, which was ridiculous because you never-- you know, what for? But that was the, the propaganda. And they-- I, I want to go back to just one more point on Theresienstadt. You know, Red Cross has done tremendous things throughout the world, and I will acknowledge that. But they came to Theresienstadt as a United Nation of some kind because I don't think United Nations was in existence in those years. But they came as a world unit to see how the Jews were treated in a ghetto in Czechoslovakia. Well, they did come. The show, which was put on for them was unheard of. Everything was painted. The band in the square was playing music on the-- on the square. They were whisked through the streets. They were whisked to the-- to the children's home, but that was a children's home of younger kids, which eventually were all gassed. But those were like kindergarten kids. And in front of the kindergarten kids-- and this is a true story-- were put sardines, and the children were taught to say, [GERMAN]. The poor children didn't even know what the sardines were. And the Red Cross blew through it, and the sardines were bl-- taken away from the children. And this was the inspection of the world of Theresienstadt. And it is absolutely unacceptable that the world was exposed because I am sure that there was on some kind of news reels somewhere being this shown. And if that was shown and the delegation which was there accepted it for what they saw, I think it was a crime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you during this inspection?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were-- we were allowed to walk the street. As a matter of fact, we were told to walk the streets in our best. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you wearing at this time? You said that you didn't get your clothes back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't but there was-- you know, the, the Jewish community has-- have got the clothes from the smaller ones to the bigger-- you know, from the bigger ones to the smaller ones and so on and so forth. So there was a sort of like a store where you would go, and you would be allotted what you needed. What you outgrew-- left the thing that you outgrew, somebody else got.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else from your time in Theresienstadt that you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, I wanted to bring to the attention of the world because if these are the, the, the commissions, as they call it, to do the inspection, well, please look a little bit deeper because this was no inspection, whatsoever. This was a showplace put up for them, and they obviously swallowed it. And they were just spooned something which did not exist.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after they left?

speaker: interviewee
text: After they left, the sardines were, of course, removed uneaten from those poor little kids. At least if they would have left them to eat it, they would have tasted it. And back to normal, which was, you know, they had the dirt and the-- and the, fleas and the, the lice and everything which got with it. And the old people, which were terribly mistreated-- in, in a way mistreated because they were old and unable and, and just there wasn't enough to sustain them. Which I think, when I'm looking forward, what I saw afterwards, and they died in Theresienstadt-- they were lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this Red Cross inspection? Do you remember the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was some time in '43, end of '43. I'm pretty sure somewhere there. Yeah, that would be right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you left Theresienstadt when, again?

speaker: interviewee
text: October '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Describe what happened, what the trip was like, what the transport was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now number one, there were a couple of transports, for sure, in front of us, which I was helping with, meaning helping the older ones to get onto the train which was brought into the ghetto. Actually , I don't even know how they did that. They got a rail shunting into, into the ghetto-- as far as I remember. I'm pretty sure they did. And there was these cattle cars with no windows, and now the SS became very evident, marching up and down by the-- by the amount of people, counting them, calling them by name into the-- into the wagons. And it was scary, very scary. A lot of people to one wag-- to one wagon. There was not room. There was just a bucket of-- for-- you know for what. And that was it. There was no nothing. No food with them, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they went-- now they had them by name. I know this because as I said, I was helping the ones who couldn't walk so well or whatever to these transports. I shouldn't have helped them. They might have made it. And now they got into the-- they must have had a list for each wagon, which was called in. Now this is the final stage, OK? And then when they got the last name, they closed the wagon door, and it was sealed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the SS continued. And if somebody was sitting down or if somebody wasn't looking the way they wanted him to look, they slapped, they screamed, they kicked, and so on. This is just about the first time I, personally, got into contact with that kind of SS. I was not. I-- and I'm repeating that-- in Theresienstadt, I was not exposed to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the train trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now that was when I was helping the two transports. This is what I saw. So when we were called up, I was pretty much ready for what was coming in that sense, only I wasn't ready for the doors to be shut on me with people just standing and being completely squeezed in an absolute position of-- you could sit, but you didn't have much room. People were-- there are claustrophobic people in, in decent circumstances. And it was screaming and yelling and screaming and yelling. I think my family was extremely disciplined. We stayed together in a corner like, and we sat down on the floor. And that was it. And when they closed the door it was very scary because it was dark. It was absolutely dark. And I think there must have been a little slit at the top of the wagon. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyhow, this finally started to move. You heard the screaming and hollering in the-- on the outside until everything was loaded. And the last door shut and, and, and sealed, and then we went. And how long? I know it went up and it went like backwards and forwards, but how long we were there would be very hard for me to even guess. Did you stop along the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stopped. We Going backwards. We stopped. We move forwards. On-- in no way was the wagon open-- at no time. So we didn't have water. We had the bucket there, and we were sitting down in the nowhere land, as I call it. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That would be about my description. I got a marvelous mechanism in my body, and I think are lots of people have learned it too. You kind of disconnect. I'm not saying that I was visualizing myself somewhere else. I mean disconnect, and I think that's what people must-- the ones who looked so disciplined in that particular train must have done because we didn't know where we were going. We didn't have no food. We were squeezed. It was mostly completely dark. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you arrived at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived somewhere, finally, and we heard enormous amount of screaming. And then we heard the doors being opened, the sealed doors. The SS was just crawling around, just absolutely crawling with bayonets and the butts of the-- of their rifles. And it was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I hope I will never ever see a picture like that because when we came finally to the door to disembark, if you can call it that, or be thrown own out from the cattle car, first of all, you didn't see properly because now you had a daylight. And it was very cold. And it was-- though the car was very stinky, this was even stinkier where we were. And all we saw was-- well, all I saw was this barbed wire with figures, not even people, standing behind the barbed wires-- skeletons with a striped uniform hanging with a cap on the head, and a face, which was not a face, and eyes, which were empty. And do you know that to this day, I have got a very hard time to look into people's eyes. And if you ask me what color of eyes somebody has, I will not know. And the reason is I came to the conclusion-- after 50 years, believe it or not-- was the shock of this absolute emptiness and nothingness of these poor creatures which were standing behind the barbed wires. Now we were thrown out of the cart-- cars-- and we had about two, three minutes with father. And there were some prisoners which were still quite able-bodied, not like the one I'm describing. And they kept on telling us, say you are older, and say you can work. That's what they kept on telling us. We didn't know what it was all about. And they were separating the women from the men. We embraced with father, and we said to him-- he said to us, this is it. We meet after the war in the apartment in Prague. We embraced. We kissed, and that was the last time I saw my father. Now there was a line of men. I never saw my father. And then there was a line, long line, of women too. I mean, this was a whole big, and this came all from Theresienstadt. Now what I want to point out is in Theresienstadt, the atmosphere was you knew everybody and you were friends or you knew them and were acquaintances or heard of them and so on and so forth. Now we are entering an era where you do not know nobody. You do not know the languages which are being spoken around you. You are like, I would imagine, a Babylon-- you know, extracted. And finishing in the line must have been another transport which I wouldn't know because all of a sudden there were people around us which we did not know. And we waited and we waited. And slowly, slowly, it was moving up. Little did I know that at the end of the line is Mengele, which we learned much, much later on. All we saw-- we held hands, mother, and my sister and myself, my mother very observant. Speaking fluent German, she observed what was happening, and what was happening was that the women-- some women-- went with the children to one side. Some children were taken out of the arms of the mothers screaming and the mother went somewhere else and the child went somewhere else. And so it went. It took hours. Finally, we came to the end of the line, and they s-- there stood an SS man. Meanwhile--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me for interrupting. We're going to pause, and we're going to continue immediately on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.
